From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

International open access database of large biological molecules

Protein Data Bank

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Content | |
| Description | * [Protein structure](/wiki/Protein_structure "Protein structure") * [X-ray crystallography](/wiki/X-ray_crystallography "X-ray crystallography") * [cryoEM](/wiki/Cryogenic_electron_microscopy "Cryogenic electron microscopy") * [NMR Structure Determination](/wiki/Nuclear_magnetic_resonance_spectroscopy_of_proteins "Nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy of proteins") |
| Contact | |
| Primary citation | [PMID](/wiki/PMID_(identifier) "PMID (identifier)") [30357364](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30357364) |
| Access | |
| [Data format](/wiki/File_format "File format") | [mmCIF](/wiki/MmCIF "MmCIF"), [PDB](/wiki/Protein_Data_Bank_(file_format) "Protein Data Bank (file format)") |
| Website | * [www.wwpdb.org](https://www.wwpdb.org/) * [ebi.ac.uk](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/pdbe/) * [www.rcsb.org](https://www.rcsb.org) * [bmrb.io](https://bmrb.io/) * [pdbj.org](https://pdbj.org/) |

The **Protein Data Bank (PDB)**[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a [database](/wiki/Database "Database") for the three-dimensional structural data of large biological molecules such as [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein") and [nucleic acids](/wiki/Nucleic_acid "Nucleic acid"), which is overseen by the [Worldwide Protein Data Bank](/wiki/Worldwide_Protein_Data_Bank "Worldwide Protein Data Bank") (wwPDB). This structural data is obtained and deposited by [biologists](/wiki/Biologist "Biologist") and [biochemists](/wiki/Biochemist "Biochemist") worldwide through the use of experimental methodologies such as [X-ray crystallography](/wiki/X-ray_crystallography "X-ray crystallography"), [NMR spectroscopy](/wiki/Nuclear_magnetic_resonance_spectroscopy_of_proteins "Nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy of proteins"), and, increasingly, [cryogenic electron microscopy](/wiki/Cryogenic_electron_microscopy "Cryogenic electron microscopy"). All submitted data are reviewed by expert [biocurators](/wiki/Biocuration "Biocuration") and, once approved, are made freely available on the Internet under the CC0 Public Domain Dedication.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Global access to the data is provided by the websites of the wwPDB member organizations (PDBe,[[3]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-3) PDBj,[[4]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-4) RCSB PDB,[[5]](#cite_note-5) BMRB[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6) and the EMDB[[7]](#cite_note-7)).

The PDB is a key in areas of [structural biology](/wiki/Structural_biology "Structural biology"), such as [structural genomics](/wiki/Structural_genomics "Structural genomics"). Most major scientific journals and some funding agencies now require scientists to submit their structure data to the PDB. Many other databases use protein structures deposited in the PDB. For example, [SCOP](/wiki/Structural_Classification_of_Proteins_database "Structural Classification of Proteins database") and [CATH](/wiki/CATH "CATH") classify protein structures, while [PDBsum](/wiki/PDBsum "PDBsum") provides a graphic overview of PDB entries using information from other sources, such as [Gene Ontology](/wiki/Gene_Ontology "Gene Ontology").[[8]](#cite_note-Historical-8)[[9]](#cite_note-pmid9433130-9)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protein_Data_Bank&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Two forces converged to initiate the PDB: a small but growing collection of sets of protein structure data determined by X-ray diffraction; and the newly available (1968) molecular graphics display, the *Brookhaven RAster Display* (BRAD), to visualize these protein structures in 3-D. In 1969, with the sponsorship of Walter Hamilton at the [Brookhaven National Laboratory](/wiki/Brookhaven_National_Laboratory "Brookhaven National Laboratory"), Edgar Meyer ([Texas A&M University](/wiki/Texas_A%26M_University "Texas A&M University")) began to write software to store atomic coordinate files in a common format to make them available for geometric and graphical evaluation. By 1971, one of Meyer's programs, SEARCH, enabled researchers to remotely access information from the database to study protein structures offline.[[10]](#cite_note-10) SEARCH was instrumental in enabling networking, thus marking the functional beginning of the PDB.

The Protein Data Bank was announced in October 1971 in [Nature New Biology](/wiki/Nature_New_Biology "Nature New Biology")[[11]](#cite_note-11) as a joint venture between [Cambridge Crystallographic Data Centre](/wiki/Cambridge_Crystallographic_Data_Centre "Cambridge Crystallographic Data Centre"), UK and Brookhaven National Laboratory, US.

Upon Hamilton's death in 1973, Tom Koetzle took over direction of the PDB for the subsequent 20 years. In January 1994, [Joel Sussman](/wiki/Joel_Sussman "Joel Sussman") of Israel's [Weizmann Institute of Science](/wiki/Weizmann_Institute_of_Science "Weizmann Institute of Science") was appointed head of the PDB. In October 1998,[[12]](#cite_note-12)
the PDB was transferred to the **Research Collaboratory for Structural Bioinformatics (RCSB)**;[[13]](#cite_note-13) the transfer was completed in June 1999. The new director was [Helen M. Berman](/wiki/Helen_M._Berman "Helen M. Berman") of [Rutgers University](/wiki/Rutgers_University "Rutgers University") (one of the managing institutions of the RCSB, the other being the [San Diego Supercomputer Center](/wiki/San_Diego_Supercomputer_Center "San Diego Supercomputer Center") at [UC San Diego](/wiki/UC_San_Diego "UC San Diego")).[[14]](#cite_note-14) In 2003, with the formation of the wwPDB, the PDB became an international organization. The founding members are PDBe (Europe),[[3]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-3) RCSB (US), and PDBj (Japan).[[4]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-4) The [Biological Magnetic Resonance Data Bank](/wiki/Biological_Magnetic_Resonance_Data_Bank "Biological Magnetic Resonance Data Bank") (BMRB)[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6) joined in 2006. The [Electron Microscopy Data Bank](/wiki/Electron_Microscopy_Data_Bank "Electron Microscopy Data Bank") (EMDB)[[15]](#cite_note-15) joined in 2021. Each of the five members of [wwPDB](/wiki/Worldwide_Protein_Data_Bank "Worldwide Protein Data Bank") can act as deposition, data processing and distribution centers for PDB data. The data processing refers to the fact that wwPDB staff review and annotate each submitted entry.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The data are then automatically checked for plausibility (the source code[[17]](#cite_note-17) for this validation software has been made available to the public at no charge).

## Contents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protein_Data_Bank&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Contents")]

The PDB database is updated weekly ([UTC](/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time "Coordinated Universal Time")+0 Wednesday), along with its holdings list.[[18]](#cite_note-18) As of 4 November 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Protein_Data_Bank&action=edit), the PDB comprised:

| Experimental Method | [Proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein") only | Proteins with oligosaccharides | Protein/Nucleic Acid complexes | [Nucleic Acids](/wiki/Nucleic_acid "Nucleic acid") only | Other | Oligosaccharides only | Total |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [X-ray diffraction](/wiki/X-ray_crystallography "X-ray crystallography") | 176378 | 10284 | 9007 | 3077 | 174 | 11 | 198,931 |
| [Electron microscopy](/wiki/Cryo-electron_microscopy "Cryo-electron microscopy") | 20438 | 3396 | 5931 | 200 | 13 | 0 | 29,978 |
| [NMR](/wiki/Nuclear_magnetic_resonance_spectroscopy_of_proteins "Nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy of proteins") | 12709 | 34 | 287 | 1554 | 33 | 6 | 14623 |
| Integrative | 342 | 8 | 24 | 2 | 3 | 0 | 379 |
| Multiple methods | 221 | 11 | 7 | 15 | 0 | 1 | 255 |
| Neutron | 83 | 1 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 0 | 87 |
| Other | 32 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 4 | 37 |
| *Total:* | 210,203 | 13,734 | 15,256 | 4,852 | 223 | 22 | 244,290 |

:   :   162,041 structures in the PDB have a [structure factor](/wiki/Structure_factor "Structure factor") file.
    :   11,242 structures have an NMR restraint file.
    :   5,774 structures in the PDB have a [chemical shifts](/wiki/Chemical_shift "Chemical shift") file.
    :   13,388 structures in the PDB have a [3DEM](/wiki/3DEM "3DEM") map file deposited in [EM Data Bank](/wiki/EM_Data_Bank "EM Data Bank")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Rate_of_Protein_Structure_Determination-2019b.png/500px-Rate_of_Protein_Structure_Determination-2019b.png)](/wiki/File:Rate_of_Protein_Structure_Determination-2019b.png)

Rate of protein structure determination techniques over the decades. MX = macromolecular [crystallography](/wiki/Crystallography "Crystallography"), 3DEM = 3D [Electron microscopy](/wiki/Electron_microscope "Electron microscope").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Most structures are determined by X-ray diffraction, but about 7% of structures are determined by [protein NMR](/wiki/Nuclear_magnetic_resonance_spectroscopy_of_proteins "Nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy of proteins"). When using X-ray diffraction, approximations of the coordinates of the atoms of the protein are obtained, whereas using NMR, the distance between pairs of atoms of the protein is estimated. The final conformation of the protein is obtained from NMR by solving a [distance geometry](/wiki/Distance_geometry "Distance geometry") problem. After 2013, a growing number of proteins are determined by [cryo-electron microscopy](/wiki/Cryo-electron_microscopy "Cryo-electron microscopy").

For PDB structures determined by X-ray diffraction that have a structure factor file, their electron density map may be viewed. The data of such structures may be viewed on the three PDB websites.

Historically, the number of structures in the PDB has grown at an approximately exponential rate, with 100 registered structures in 1982, 1,000 structures in 1993, 10,000 in 1999, 100,000 in 2014, and 200,000 in January 2023.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### PDB-IHM

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protein_Data_Bank&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: PDB-IHM")]

PDB-Dev was a database also managed by wwPDB, for structural models arising from a "integrative" or "hybrid" approach, i.e. combining experiment and structure prediction. Included models use the same four-character accession code format. In 2024, PDB-Dev was renamed to PDB-IHM and unified into the PDB: its structures can now be accessed from regular wwPDB endpoints including websites. As of January 2026, PDB-IHM contains 382 entries.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## File format

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protein_Data_Bank&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: File format")]

Main article: [Protein Data Bank (file format)](/wiki/Protein_Data_Bank_(file_format) "Protein Data Bank (file format)")

The file format initially used by the PDB was called the PDB file format. The original format was restricted by the width of [computer punch cards](/wiki/Computer_punch_card "Computer punch card") to 80 characters per line. Around 1996, the "macromolecular Crystallographic Information file" format, mmCIF, which is an extension of the [CIF format](/wiki/Crystallographic_Information_File "Crystallographic Information File") was phased in. mmCIF became the standard format for the PDB archive in 2014.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In 2019, the wwPDB announced that depositions for crystallographic methods would only be accepted in mmCIF format.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

An [XML](/wiki/XML "XML") version of PDB, called PDBML, was described in 2005.[[25]](#cite_note-25)
The structure files can be downloaded in any of these three formats, though an increasing number of structures do not fit the legacy PDB format. Individual files are easily downloaded into graphics packages from Internet [URLs](/wiki/URL "URL"):

* For PDB format files, use, e.g., `http://www.pdb.org/pdb/files/4hhb.pdb.gz` or `http://pdbe.org/download/4hhb`
* For PDBML (XML) files, use, e.g., `http://www.pdb.org/pdb/files/4hhb.xml.gz` or `http://pdbe.org/pdbml/4hhb`

The "`4hhb`" is the PDB identifier. Each structure published in PDB receives a four-character alphanumeric identifier, its PDB ID. (This is not a unique identifier for biomolecules, because several structures for the same molecule—in different environments or conformations—may be contained in PDB with different PDB IDs.)

## Viewing the data

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protein_Data_Bank&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Viewing the data")]

The structure files may be viewed using one of [several free and open source computer programs](/wiki/List_of_open_source_software_packages#Molecule_viewer "List of open source software packages"), including [Jmol](/wiki/Jmol "Jmol"), [Pymol](/wiki/PyMOL "PyMOL"), [VMD](/wiki/Visual_Molecular_Dynamics "Visual Molecular Dynamics"), [Molstar](/wiki/Molstar "Molstar") and [Rasmol](/wiki/RasMol "RasMol"). Other non-free, [shareware](/wiki/Shareware "Shareware") programs include ICM-Browser,[[26]](#cite_note-26) [MDL Chime](/wiki/MDL_Chime "MDL Chime"), [UCSF Chimera](/wiki/UCSF_Chimera "UCSF Chimera"), Swiss-PDB Viewer,[[27]](#cite_note-27) StarBiochem[[28]](#cite_note-28) (a Java-based interactive molecular viewer with integrated search of protein databank), [Sirius](/wiki/Sirius_visualization_software "Sirius visualization software"), and VisProt3DS[[29]](#cite_note-29) (a tool for Protein Visualization in 3D stereoscopic view in anaglyph and other modes), and [Discovery Studio](/wiki/Discovery_Studio "Discovery Studio"). The RCSB PDB website contains an extensive list of both free and commercial molecule visualization programs and web browser plugins.

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protein_Data_Bank&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Crystallographic database](/wiki/Crystallographic_database "Crystallographic database")
* [Databank](/wiki/Databank "Databank")
* [Protein structure](/wiki/Protein_structure "Protein structure")
* [Protein structure prediction](/wiki/Protein_structure_prediction "Protein structure prediction")
* [Protein structure database](/wiki/Protein_structure_database "Protein structure database")
* [PDBREPORT](/wiki/PDBREPORT "PDBREPORT") lists all anomalies (also errors) in PDB structures
* [PDBsum](/wiki/PDBsum "PDBsum")—extracts data from other databases about PDB structures
* [Proteopedia](/wiki/Proteopedia "Proteopedia")—a collaborative 3D encyclopedia of proteins and other molecules
